title: Function pointers and callbacks

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Function pointers and callbacks; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. canonicalExplanation.summary: A function pointer has a compatible function type and can select behavior at runtime, but calls remain valid only when return and parameter types match the pointed-to function.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A transform function receives int (*operation)(int) and calls either square or negate through the compatible pointer. Your goal is to implement and explain a small callback-based operation without incompatible casts. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: One-dimensional arrays; Parameters and return values; Addresses and dereference; Declarations, prototypes and definitions. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: A function pointer has a compatible function type and can select behavior at runtime, but calls remain valid only when return and parameter types match the pointed-to function. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Object and function pointers are not generally interchangeable, casts do not repair incompatible call types, and callback context often needs a separate void pointer contract. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Casting any function address to the requested pointer type makes an incompatible callback call safe.

includes: Store compatible function addresses and invoke callbacks through typed interfaces.
Implement and explain a small callback-based operation without incompatible casts.

outcomes: Implement and explain a small callback-based operation without incompatible casts.

examples.title: Function pointers and callbacks: defined case
examples.situation: A transform function receives int (*operation)(int) and calls either square or negate through the compatible pointer.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A transform function receives int (*operation)(int) and calls either square or negate through the compatible pointer.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: The actual function and pointer call type must be compatible; a cast can silence a diagnostic but cannot create a valid calling convention.
examples.result: The defined result follows because The actual function and pointer call type must be compatible; a cast can silence a diagnostic but cannot create a valid calling convention.

examples.title: Function pointers and callbacks: boundary case
examples.situation: A comparison callback uses the exact library-required signature and converts context data only under a documented object-pointer contract.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A comparison callback uses the exact library-required signature and converts context data only under a documented object-pointer contract.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Object and function pointers are not generally interchangeable, casts do not repair incompatible call types, and callback context often needs a separate void pointer contract.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Casting any function address to the requested pointer type makes an incompatible callback call safe.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement and explain a small callback-based operation without incompatible casts.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Casting any function address to the requested pointer type makes an incompatible callback call safe.
misconceptions.correction: The actual function and pointer call type must be compatible; a cast can silence a diagnostic but cannot create a valid calling convention.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: Casting any function address to the requested pointer type makes an incompatible callback call safe.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Function pointers and callbacks: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
Object and function pointers are not generally interchangeable, casts do not repair incompatible call types, and callback context often needs a separate void pointer contract.
